item.title: Re: Audio Adaptation incoming, but...
item.content:encoded: Still nothing years after… I would expect it to be in the 4th paragraph but nothing stands out!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like this should be doing something for me. At the end of the day, it's neat, but just too lacking in substance.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This does nothing for me. No fridge horror, no lingering unsettling feeling, no impressions. The effect of the SCP seems forced and weak. I feel like I'm going to forget this in a minute.

No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was curious about the actual count too, and checked word. I got 502. After some checking, Word counts the "#" in the item number as a word, as well as the "-" in the third paragraph of the description, after the word "degradation". Take out those two symbol/words and Word counts 500 words exactly.

item.title: Audio Adaptation incoming, but...
item.content:encoded: I got the same four out of place words as Xaser did. I've read this out loud several times and cannot find a fifth out of place word. I really want to know what the fifth word is before posting an audio script; can anyone find this sucker?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
… Wait a second.
What does this thing do to the index? o_o

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Late, but I can't figure out which the last one is; it doesn't stand out as sorely as the other four do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am a fan of gimmicks and format screws, but this wasn't interesting.

I thought the sentence "The second effect happens if the word count of the text is over 500 donkeys" was genuinely funny, but inappropriate word insertion jokes have been done. Repeatedly. For years.

If it had been something super weird rather than a simplistic object, it would still have been formulaic, but it could have actually been entertaining. It's not hard to imagine something near-incomprehensible, that the Foundation would normally write thousands of words about and still not understand — like Star Signals (but not necessarily horrifying) — that also forces you to only write 500 words about it.

Like some sort of tiny obsessive-compulsive beetle god forcing its article to conform to its strict standards. Or a word count error afflicting the Foundation database due to an attempt to catalogue something you simply cannot record in a computer system because it's too eldritch to understand (color out of space style). A nearly successful corruption of the Foundation's entire database by the Insurgency using techno-metamagic, contained to just this article (about itself). A bored star. A homework assignment from the Buried Giant's strict English teacher. Or something regarding the number 500. Did you know that 500 kilohertz is the distress frequency for Morse code communication? Or that 500 BC is approximately when the world's population reached 100 million for the first time? If you didn't, you could make stuff up. Nothing at all wrong with a metajoke about how 500 is some kind of divinely favored number — or an infernally favored number. (Representing God? Some other, frightening form of God?) Hell, even in older canon, SCP-500 is a miracle cure which later fell out of favor. That's got some easy thematic implications if you want them. There are many other possible ideas.

Instead, you wrote something that's less interesting than many entries on the Anomalous Objects List.

And why? It doesn't really benefit the article from a reader experience POV to make it more simple. It's not a better story by being more simple. It's not a more fun puzzle by being more simple. It's easier to write, but…

This is especially a shame because clearly people find the puzzle aspect fascinating, even if the puzzle is limited to 'find the five out-of-place words'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No, monthly is supposed to be there.

Keep looking ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm, taking a quick look-through…

+ maybe spoilers
- words

violently
costing
donkeys
ground
monthly


Did I miss one?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was word counted using the word counter used for the Short Works contest, specifically this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I count 502 words, Or at least MSWord does when I copy paste the document.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So this got translated i feel so special

http://scpfoundation.ru/scp-2383

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's taking the parameters of the contest as a starting point, and then there's unashamedly mugging for upvotes by doing that "wink wink nod nod" thing extremely hard. To the point of referencing staff members.

It's nothing but gimmick, as far as I can see.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
would the Foundation let the document get that many words below the 500 forced total, when that degrades the info in the text?

Hadn't thought of that. Good point!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seems I've misread that sentence three or four times now!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nope; if you remove the word "donkeys" from the text, it still reads grammatically correctly.

The second effect happens when the word count of the text is over 500.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One thing that bothers me: the donkey seems to be a replacement rather than an addition. A word goes there, but now it's a nonsense word. Is this an extra effect that hasn't been noted yet?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I get what you mean, but when I though about that it presented another problem in-universe; would the Foundation let the document get that many words below the 500 forced total, when that degrades the info in the text? I felt that merely 5 words less was largely stretching the suspension of disbelief of what the Foundation would allow, since even that caused some slight noticeable degradation of the information.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Heh heh. Let's put it to the test!

Though it doesn't change my vote, I quite agree with Kapitano:

I think it would be a better joke if, instead of the document getting more waffly as it goes on, sentences could gain more out-of-place donkey words.

Would be funny indeed!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the meta and upvoted for it. That said, I'm not sure this would work outside the context of this contest, or if it will have staying power once the contest is over.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That may have been that I had to change a bit and forgot to change the plural of the verb. Oops.
(but still thanks for liking yay)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Again, I give no points for meta or for gimmicks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's…anomalous filler. Preternatural padding.

I think it would be a better joke if, instead of the document getting more waffly as it goes on, sentences could gain more out-of-place donkey words.

And/or the final sentence cut of before finishing, when it gets to 500 words.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it. Especially because it's difficult to tell actual typos or mistakes from anomalous effects… Well done!!!!

This one, for instance:

some texts concerning SCP-2383 is still, albeit rarely, found in various locations

It should be 'some texts are still found'
Or should it??????

Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was clever, and it made me reread the document a few times to catch changes. I appreciate that little bit of effort. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The donkey bit made me giggle, but it feels less like an in-universe anomaly and more like a forced and convenient way for the author to fit the rules of the contest. No vote, as it is pretty amusing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On one hand, it's a fun gimmick which is played off well, without being too on the nose. On the other, I feel if this had gone a bit less meta and lowered the cap, it would have been far more readable. I know that it's supposed to be tedious, redundant, and likely obnoxious, but at the same time… it does those things a bit too straight at times.

+0

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really well done. I love that it's played straight with the errant "donkey".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's another part of the meta.

Think about who's running the contest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, I like your attitude towards this contest. This seems like what I would have written for it, if I didn't try for something serious.

That being said, though… my understanding is that the gist is that the article's word count was artificially bumped up by the anomaly to hit the 500 mark, resulting in an article with a lot of filler. But, while it is justified in the article, pieces with lots of filler text are still not fun to read!

I think this would have been better if you had taken the approach of trying, and obviously failing, to condense too much information into 500 words. Then it could have done something else interesting, maybe, and while it wouldn't technically be good writing still, there would at least be value in every word.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do not understand why it's a light bulb. Idea symbolism?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
violently

This is part of the SCP's adding in the extra words.

"replace" -> "replaced"

I don't completely agree, I've added in a comma to hopefully make it more clear. "The word count is to be counted, and replace the lower of the two numbers…"

The last one

Hrm, I see what you mean, I've switched around the word order to (hopefully) make it more clear.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

All other texts referencing SCP-2383 are to be destroyed violently

Violently? Is there a standard procedure to destroy them? From how it's presented, it feels like one can destroy via any means (be it excessive or "not enough").

is to be counted and replace

"replace" –> "replaced"

some text concerning SCP-2383 is still, albeit very rarely, found in locations,

I don't really understand this portion's syntax. What are these locations? I know that those further apart are affected less. But I'm not sure what the quoted statement is trying to convey.

Unless it's part of the anomalous effect?

Pros:

  • It's fairly meta. However, unlike other forms of meta SCPs, this one is relatively subtle and can pass off as a normal text.
  • I like the implication that there are 5 extra words added into the mix, so you can't really trust the document at face value.

Cons:

  • As mentioned above, readability falters slightly at one point… which didn't help my reading experience.

Verdict: Upvote
